## EXPRESSIVENESS AND APPROXIMATION PROPERTIES OF GRAPH NEURAL NETWORKS

## **Floris Geerts**

Department of Computer Science, University of Antwerp, Belgium floris.geerts@uantwerpen.be

## **Juan L. Reutter**

School of Engineering, Pontificia Universidad Cat´olica de Chile, Chile & IMFD, Chile jreutter@ing.puc.cl

## ABSTRACT

Characterizing the separation power of graph neural networks (GNNs) provides an understanding of their limitations for graph learning tasks. Results regarding separation power are, however, usually geared at specific GNN architectures, and tools for understanding arbitrary GNN architectures are generally lacking. We provide an elegant way to easily obtain bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of the Weisfeiler-Leman (WL) tests, which have become the yardstick to measure the separation power of GNNs. The crux is to view GNNs as expressions in a procedural tensor language describing the computations in the layers of the GNNs. Then, by a simple analysis of the obtained expressions, in terms of the number of indexes and the nesting depth of summations, bounds on the separation power in terms of the WL-tests readily follow. We use tensor language to define Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs. Furthermore, the tensor language point of view allows for the derivation of universality results for classes of GNNs in a natural way. Our approach provides a toolbox with which GNN architecture designers can analyze the separation power of their GNNs, without needing to know the intricacies of the WL-tests. We also provide insights in what is needed to boost the separation power of GNNs.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) (Merkwirth & Lengauer, 2005; Scarselli et al., 2009) cover many popular deep learning methods for graph learning tasks (see Hamilton (2020) for a recent overview). These methods typically compute vector embeddings of vertices or graphs by relying on the underlying adjacency information. Invariance (for graph embeddings) and equivariance (for vertex embeddings) of GNNs ensure that these methods are oblivious to the precise representation of the graphs.

**Separation power.** Our primary focus is on the separation power of GNN architectures, i.e., on their ability to separate vertices or graphs by means of the computed embeddings. It has become standard to characterize GNN architectures in terms of the separation power of graph algorithms such as color refinement (CR) and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman tests ( _k_ -WL), as initiated in Xu et al. (2019) and Morris et al. (2019). Unfortunately, understanding the separation power of any given GNN architecture requires complex proofs, geared at the specifics of the architecture. We provide a _tensor language-based technique_ to analyze the separation power of general GNNs.

**Tensor languages.** Matrix query languages (Brijder et al., 2019; Geerts et al., 2021b) are defined to assess the expressive power of linear algebra. Balcilar et al. (2021a) observe that, by casting various GNNs into the MATLANG (Brijder et al., 2019) matrix query language, one can use existing separation results (Geerts, 2021) to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of GNNs in terms of 1-WL and 2-WL. In this paper, we considerably extend this approach by defining, and studying, a new general-purpose tensor language specifically designed for modeling GNNs. As in Balcilar et al. (2021a), our focus on tensor languages allows us to obtain new insights about GNN architectures.

1

Published as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

First, since tensor languages can only define invariant and equivariant graph functions, any GNN that can be cast in our tensor language inherits these desired properties. More importantly, the separation power of our tensor language is as closely related to CR and _k_ -WL as GNNs are. Loosely speaking, _if tensor language expressions use k_ + 1 _indices, then their separation power is bounded by k-_ WL _. Furthermore, if the maximum nesting of summations in the expression is t, then t rounds of k-_ WL _are needed to obtain an upper bound on the separation power._ A similar connection is obtained for CR and a fragment of tensor language that we call “guarded” tensor language.

We thus reduce problem of assessing the separation power of _any_ specific GNN architecture to the problem of specifying it in our tensor language, analyzing the number of indices used and counting their summation depth. This is usually much easier than dealing with intricacies of CR and _k_ -WL, as casting GNNs in our tensor language is often as simple as writing down their layer-based definition. We believe that this provides a nice toolbox for GNN designers to assess the separation power of their architecture. We use this toolbox to recover known results about the separation power of specific GNN architectures such as GINs (Xu et al., 2019), GCNs (Kipf & Welling, 2017), Folklore GNNs (Maron et al., 2019b), _k_ -GNNs (Morris et al., 2019), and several others. We also derive new results: we answer an open problem posed by Maron et al. (2019a) by showing that the separation power of Invariant Graph Networks ( _k_ -IGNs), introduced by Maron et al. (2019b), is bounded by ( _k −_ 1)-WL. In addition, we revisit the analysis by Balcilar et al. (2021b) of ChebNet (Defferrard et al., 2016), and show that CayleyNet (Levie et al., 2019) is bounded by 2-WL.

When writing down GNNs in our tensor language, the less indices needed, the stronger the bounds in terms of _k_ -WL we obtain. After all, ( _k −_ 1)-WL is known to be strictly less separating than _k_ -WL (Otto, 2017). Thus, it is important to minimize the number of indices used in tensor language expressions. We connect this number to the notion of _treewidth_ : expressions of treewidth _k_ can be translated into expressions using _k_ + 1 indices. This corresponds to optimizing expressions, as done in many areas in machine learning, by reordering the summations (a.k.a. variable elimination).

**Approximation and universality.** We also consider the ability of GNNs to approximate general invariant or equivariant graph functions. Once more, instead of focusing on specific architectures, we use our tensor languages to obtain general approximation results, which naturally translate to universality results for GNNs. We show: ( _k_ + 1)-index tensor language expressions suffice to approximate any (invariant/equivariant) graph function whose separating power is bounded by _k_ -WL, and we can further refine this by comparing the number of rounds in _k_ -WL with the summation depth of the expressions. These results provide a finer picture than the one obtained by Azizian & Lelarge (2021). Furthermore, focusing on “guarded” tensor expressions yields a similar universality result for CR, a result that, to our knowledge, was not known before. We also provide the link between approximation results for tensor expressions and GNNs, enabling us to transfer our insights into universality properties of GNNs. As an example, we show that _k_ -IGNs can approximate any graph function that is less separating than ( _k−_ 1)-WL. This case was left open in Azizian & Lelarge (2021).

In summary, we draw new and interesting connections between tensor languages, GNN architectures and classic graph algorithms. We provide a general recipe to bound the separation power of GNNs, optimize them, and understand their approximation power. We show the usefulness of our method by recovering several recent results, as well as new results, some of them left open in previous work.

**Related work.** Separation power has been studied for specific classes of GNNs (Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019; Maron et al., 2019b; Chen et al., 2019; Morris et al., 2020; Azizian & Lelarge, 2021). A first general result concerns the bounds in terms of CR and 1-WL of Message-Passing Neural Networks (Gilmer et al., 2017; Morris et al., 2019; Xu et al., 2019). Balcilar et al. (2021a) use the MATLANG matrix query language to obtain upper bounds on the separation power of various GNNs. MATLANG can only be used to obtain bounds up to 2-WL and is limited to matrices. Our tensor language is more general and flexible and allows for reasoning over the number of indices, treewidth, and summation depth of expressions. These are all crucial for our main results. The tensor language introduced resembles sum-MATLANG (Geerts et al., 2021b), but with the added ability to represent tensors. Neither separation power nor guarded fragments were considered in Geerts et al. (2021b). See Section A in the supplementary material for more details. For universality, Azizian & Lelarge (2021) is closest in spirit. Our approach provides an elegant way to recover and extend their results. Azizian & Lelarge (2021) describe how their work (and hence also ours) encompasses previous works (Keriven & Peyr´e, 2019; Maron et al., 2019c; Chen et al., 2019). Our results use connections between _k_ -WL and logics (Immerman & Lander, 1990; Cai et al., 1992), and CR and

## 3 SPECIFYING GNNS

Many GNNs use linear algebra computations on vectors, matrices or tensors, interleaved with the application of activation functions or MLPs. To understand the separation power of GNNs, we introduce a specification language, TL, for tensor language, that allows us to specify any algebraic computation in a procedural way by explicitly stating how each entry is to be computed. We gauge the separation power of GNNs by specifying them as TL expressions, and syntactically analyzing the components of such TL expressions. This technique gives rise to _Higher-Order Message-Passing Neural Networks_ (or _k_ -MPNNs), a natural extension of MPNNs (Gilmer et al., 2017). For simplicity, we present TL using summation aggregation only but arbitrary aggregation functions on multisets of real values can be used as well (Section C.5 in the supplementary material).

## 4 SEPARATION POWER OF TENSOR LANGUAGES

Our first main results concern the characterization of the separation power of tensor languages in terms of the color refinement and _k_ -dimensional Weisfeiler-Leman algorithms. We provide a finegrained characterization by taking the number of rounds of these algorithms into account. This will allow for measuring the separation power of classes of GNNs in terms of their number of layers.

## 4.2 MAIN RESULTS

We first provide a link between _k_ -WL and tensor language expressions using _k_ + 1 index variables: **Theorem 4.1.** _For each k ≥_ 1 _and any collection_ Ω _of functions, ρ_ 1�vwl _[(] k[∞][)]_ � = _ρ_ 1�TL _k_ +1(Ω)� _._

This theorem gives us new insights: if we wish to understand how a new GNN architecture compares against the _k_ -WL algorithms, all we need to do is to show that such an architecture can be represented in TL _k_ +1(Ω), i.e., is a _k_ -MPNN, an arguably much easier endeavor. As an example of how to use this result, it is well known that triangles can be detected by 2-WL but not by 1-WL. Thus, in order to design GNNs that can detect triangles, layer definitions in TL3 rather than TL2 should be used.
We can do much more, relating the rounds of _k_ -WL to the notion of summation depth of TL(Ω) expressions. We also present present similar results for functions computing graph embeddings.

We also consider “augmented” GNNs, which are combined with a preprocessing step in which higher-order graph information is computed. In the supplementary material (Section D.3) we show how TL encodes the preprocessing step, and how this leads to separation bounds in terms of _k_ -WL, where _k_ depends on the treewidth of the graph information used. ## 7 CONCLUSION

Connecting GNNs and tensor languages allows us to use our analysis of tensor languages to understand the separation and approximation power of GNNs. The number of indices and summation depth needed to represent the layers in GNNs determine their separation power in terms of color refinement and Weisfeiler-Leman tests. The framework of _k_ -MPNNs provides a handy toolbox to understand existing and new GNN architectures, and we demonstrate this by recovering several results about the power of GNNs presented recently in the literature, as well as proving new results.

zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.label: Zero Shot (claude-opus-4.7)
zero_shot__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents an elegant framework for analyzing the separation and approximation power of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) via a specially-designed tensor language (TL). The core idea — that the number of index variables and summation depth of TL expressions directly relates to the k-WL hierarchy and rounds thereof — is well-motivated and yields a useful toolbox. The paper successfully applies this framework to recover known results (GINs, GCNs, k-FGNNs) and to establish new ones (e.g., the (k-1)-WL bound for k-IGNs, answering an open problem). The presentation is mostly clear, though some technical details are deferred to supplementary material. Below I note a few issues mainly relating to notational clarity, precision of claims, and potential ambiguity in definitions.

comments.title: Atomic type dimension inconsistency
comments.quote: its _atomic type_ in _G_ , denoted by atp _k_ ( _G,_ _**v**_ ), is a vector in R[2][(] _[k]_ 2[)][+] _[kℓ]_ . The first  _k_ 2 entries are 0 _/_ 1-values encoding the equality type of _**v**_ , i.e., whether _vi_ = _vj_ for 1 _≤ i < j ≤ k_ .
comments.explanation: The dimension is stated as R^{2(k choose 2) + kℓ}, which means 2·(k choose 2) + kℓ real-valued entries. This would correctly correspond to (k choose 2) equality-type entries plus (k choose 2) adjacency entries plus kℓ label entries. So the total dimension matches. However, for k=1 we have (1 choose 2) = 0, so the atomic type reduces to a vector in R^ℓ containing only col_G(v). This is consistent but worth noting: when k=1 there is no equality information or adjacency information, which is why the paper later distinguishes 1-WL from CR (CR doesn't use non-adjacency information). The definition is technically correct but the presentation doesn't flag the edge case, which could confuse readers trying to verify the formula.

comments.title: Definition of wl with u inserted at position other than 1..k unclear
comments.explanation: The construction of M includes atp_{k+1}(G, vu) — a (k+1)-tuple by appending u to v. The use of (k+1)-tuple atomic types inside a k-WL definition is correct for folklore k-WL but relies on the convention where the atomic type of the extended tuple captures the relationship between u and each v_i. However, the formula doesn't explicitly say that (k+1)-WL's atomic type is being used here; readers unfamiliar with the notation may wonder why the atomic type appears with k+1. The definition is standard but slightly terse — a brief clarifying remark would help reproducibility, but this is a minor notational concern since the paper explicitly states this is the 'folklore' k-WL of Cai et al. (1992).

comments.title: Invariance/equivariance notation mismatch
comments.quote: A function _f_ : _G_ 0 _→_ F is called _invariant_ if _f_ ( _G_ ) = _f_ ( _σ ⋆G_ ) for any permutation _π_ .
comments.explanation: The text uses 'permutation π' but the permutation symbol everywhere else is σ (e.g., 'σ ⋆ G'). This is a trivial inconsistency (likely a typo) that's easily resolved by context, but it could briefly confuse readers checking the definition carefully. Since the same definition uses σ and π in nearby lines, this is a small notational slip.

comments.title: Ambiguity in semantics of summation in TL
comments.explanation: The semantics is defined relative to a valuation ν that assigns a vertex to EACH index variable, but expressions may have free variables. It's implied that the valuation domain includes at least all free variables plus bound variables introduced by Σ_x. The definition of Σ_x explicitly extends ν via ν[x ↦ v]. However, how the 'initial' valuation should be provided (partial valuations for free vars only?) is not fully explicit. In the later equation [[φ(x),v]]_G = [[φ(x), x↦v]]_G this is clarified by context, so a careful reader will reconstruct it, but the handling of partial valuations when expressions contain both free and bound variables is left implicit. Minor ambiguity but resolvable.

comments.title: Guarded TL restricted to 2 variables but claims extend to k
comments.quote: The _guarded fragment_ GTL(Ω) of TL2(Ω) is inspired by the use of adjacency matrices in simple GNNs. In GTL(Ω) only equality predicates **1** _xi_ = _xi_ (constant 1) and **1** _xi_ = _xi_ (constant 0) are allowed
comments.explanation: The guarded fragment is defined as a subset of TL_2(Ω), and summation must occur via Σ_{x_j}(E(x_i,x_j)·φ(x_j)) for i,j ∈ [2]. However, the text also uses 'equality predicates 1_{xi=xi} (constant 1) and 1_{xi=xi} (constant 0)' — the second should presumably be 1_{xi ≠ xi} for constant 0, since x_i = x_i is always true. This appears to be a typo/OCR error where both predicates are written identically. A reader could easily deduce the intent (one equality, one disequality), but this should be flagged as a typographical error that affects clarity of the formal definition.

comments.title: Claim about functions not adding expressive power needs qualification
comments.quote: All separation results for TL(Ω) and fragments thereof hold irregardless of the chosen functions in Ω, including when no functions are present at all. Function applications hence do not add expressive power.
comments.explanation: This statement is phrased as if functions in Ω don't add separation power at all, but strictly speaking, the claim is about upper bounds matching lower bounds determined by the number of indices and summation depth. The discussion would benefit from nuance: functions can still change WHICH features (vectors in R^ℓ) are computed, and specific activation functions may matter for approximation power (as discussed in Section 6). The claim is technically correct about SEPARATION (equivalence classes), but the bald statement 'function applications hence do not add expressive power' could mislead readers about approximation power. Section 6 partially addresses this, but the standalone phrasing here is a mild overclaim.

comments.title: Treewidth proposition stated without definition in main text
comments.quote: **Proposition 4.5.** _Expressions in_ TL(Ω) _of treewidth k are equivalent to expressions in_ TL _k_ +1(Ω) _._
comments.explanation: The notion of 'treewidth of a TL expression' is central to Proposition 4.5 and to the subsequent discussion about going beyond k-WL, but the definition is deferred to the supplementary material (Section G). The main text gives some intuition via the example θ(x_1), but a precise definition is absent. This makes it hard for readers to verify the claim about treewidth of E(x_1,x_2)·E(x_2,x_3)·E(x_3,x_4) being one versus the 3-clique version being two. Given the importance of this concept to the main narrative of the paper (recipe #4), including at least a brief formal definition in the main text would strengthen the exposition. This is a minor issue since readers familiar with treewidth of conjunctive queries/hypergraphs can reconstruct it, but it's worth flagging.

comments.title: Theorem 5.1 bound 'tk rounds' not fully motivated in main text
comments.quote: **Theorem 5.1.** _For any k ≥_ 1 _, the separation power of a t-layered_ ( _k_ + 1) _-_ IGN _s is bounded by the separation power of tk rounds of k-_ WL _._
comments.explanation: The theorem states a tk-round bound. The paper explains 'The additional rounds (tk) are needed because (k+1)-IGNs aggregate information in one layer that becomes accessible to k-WL in k rounds.' This intuition is helpful but the proof is deferred to supplementary material. Given that this is claimed as a main new result answering an open problem from Maron et al. (2019a), a reader wanting to verify whether the factor k is tight or if it could be improved is left without a clear path. Also, the prior work Chen et al. (2020) is cited as having solved only the k=1 case 'by analyzing properties of 1-WL' — the paper could state whether their k=1 result gave t rounds or tk=t rounds, since for k=1 these coincide. Minor framing issue.

zero_shot__gpt-5.5.label: Zero Shot (gpt-5.5)
zero_shot__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper presents a strong and useful framework: encoding GNN computations in a tensor language gives a clean route to WL-style separation bounds, and most of the main technical narrative is coherent. The main concerns I found are not with the central tensor-language idea, but with several places where formal statements are either too strong or not quite aligned with the surrounding definitions. In particular, the approximation theorem appears to omit the usual continuity restriction required by Stone-Weierstrass-type arguments, and the later approximation consequences mix vertex-level and graph-level WL/CR notation in ways that can make the stated universality claims formally incorrect. There are also a few localized notation/formula issues in the background and guarded-fragment definition that are probably easy to repair but should be corrected because they occur in formal statements.

comments.title: Approximation theorem appears to omit the required continuity restriction
comments.quote: **Theorem 6.1.** _For any ℓ, and any set F of functions, concatenation and function closed for ℓ, we have: Fℓ_ = _{f_ : _Gs →_ R _[ℓ] | ρs_ ( _F_ ) _⊆ ρs_ ( _f_ ) _}._
comments.explanation: The surrounding discussion correctly invokes a generalized Stone-Weierstrass theorem and assumes compactness of the input space, which is the standard route for proving density/universality results. However, as stated, the right-hand side contains all functions that are constant on the equivalence classes induced by F, not just continuous ones. That cannot be correct in general. For example, if F separates points on a compact continuum such as a label space K ⊆ R, then ρs(F) is equality, so the displayed right-hand side would include every arbitrary function on K, including discontinuous functions. But a uniform closure of continuous TL/GNN-realizable functions, or of functions closed under continuous postcomposition, cannot contain discontinuous targets. The intended statement is presumably about continuous functions f : Gs → R^[ℓ] that are no more separating than F, or else it needs additional assumptions allowing discontinuous generators. This is a central approximation claim, so the missing continuity qualifier materially affects the theorem and its later consequences.

comments.title: Strict inclusion claims for WL/CR rounds are overstated
comments.quote: The following strict inclusions are known: for all _k ≥_ 1, _ρ_ 1(vwl[(] _k[t]_ +1[)][)] _[ ⊂][ρ]_[1][(][vwl] _k_[(] _[t]_[)][)][ and] _[ ρ]_[1][(][vwl] 1[(] _[t]_[)][)] _[ ⊂][ρ]_[1][(][cr][(] _[t]_[)][)][ (][Otto][,][ 2017][;][ Grohe][,][ 2021][).] It is also known that more rounds ( _t_ ) increase the separation power of these algorithms (F¨urer, 2001).
comments.explanation: I understand the intended point: more WL rounds and the unguarded 1-WL variant can be more separating than CR, and there are standard hierarchy/separation results. But the displayed strict inclusions, as written, are too strong. First, at t = 0 the paper’s own definitions give cr^(0)(G,v) = col_G(v), while vwl_1^(0)(G,v) is the 1-tuple atomic type, which for simple vertex-labelled graphs contains only the vertex label because there are no equality or adjacency pairs for k = 1. Thus ρ1(vwl_1^(0)) and ρ1(cr^(0)) coincide, not strictly include one another. Second, earlier the paper fixes graph size n; for fixed n, WL procedures stabilize after finitely many rounds, so ρ1(vwl_k^(t+1)) ⊂ ρ1(vwl_k^(t)) cannot hold for all t. The standard result is better phrased as monotonic refinement, with strictness witnessed for suitable graph sizes/instances and ranges of t, not as a uniform strict inclusion for every fixed n and t.

comments.title: Guarded TL definition gives the same equality predicate as both constant 1 and constant 0
comments.quote: In GTL(Ω) only equality predicates **1** _xi_ = _xi_ (constant 1) and **1** _xi_ = _xi_ (constant 0) are allowed, addition and multiplication require the component expressions to have the same (single) free index, and summation must occur in a _guarded form_[] _xj_  _E_ ( _xi, xj_ ) _· φ_ ( _xj_ ), for _i, j ∈_ [2].
comments.explanation: The intent of the guarded fragment is clear: allow only trivial equality/disequality tests and neighbor-guarded summation, matching message passing over edges. However, the formal description says that the same predicate **1**_{xi = xi} is both constant 1 and constant 0. Given the earlier TL syntax with op ∈ {=, ≠}, the second predicate was presumably meant to be **1**_{xi ≠ xi}, which is indeed constantly 0. This is likely just a local typo, but it appears inside the definition of GTL, so a reader trying to reconstruct the fragment exactly would encounter a contradiction.

comments.title: Approximation consequences mix vertex-level and graph-level algorithms
comments.quote: Together with our results in Section 4, the corollary implies that MPNNs[(] _[t]_[)] , 1-MPNNs[(] _[t]_[)] , _k_ -MPNNs[(] _[t]_[)] or _k_ -MPNNs can approximate all functions with equal or less separation power than cr[(] _[t]_[)] , gcr[(] _[t]_[)] , vwl[(] _k[t]_[)][or][ gwl][(] _k[∞]_[)] , respectively.
comments.explanation: The intended message is sensible: once a GNN class has the same separation relation as the appropriate WL/CR procedure and is closed under concatenation and MLPs, its closure contains exactly the functions that are no more separating than that procedure. But the correspondences in this sentence do not line up with the definitions given earlier. MPNNs^(t) were introduced as guarded vertex-embedding functions and should correspond to cr^(t). Unguarded 1-MPNNs^(t), as vertex functions, should correspond to vwl_1^(t), not gcr^(t), which is a graph-level color-refinement readout. Conversely, gcr^(t) is relevant for graph embeddings/readouts, where summation depth shifts also matter. Similarly, the final item “k-MPNNs” is ambiguous between vertex-level vwl_k^(∞) and graph-level gwl_k^(∞). The surrounding sections contain the ingredients to state this correctly, but this sentence collapses distinct vertex and graph settings and may lead readers to apply the wrong equivalence relation.

comments.title: Graph-level approximation statement uses vwl where gwl is required
comments.quote: and when extended with a readout layer, their closures consist of functions _f_ : _G_ 0 _→_ R _[ℓ]_ satisfying _ρ_ 0(gcr[(] _[t]_[)] ) = _ρ_ 0(vwl[(] 1 _[t]_[)][)] _[⊆][ρ]_[0][(] _[f]_[)][.
comments.explanation: Here the discussion is explicitly about graph-level functions f : G0 → R^[ℓ] obtained after a readout layer. Earlier, the paper carefully distinguishes vertex labels vwl_k^(t) : G1 → F from graph labels gwl_k^(t) : G0 → F, and defines ρ0 only for graph functions. Thus ρ0(vwl_1^(t)) is not defined under the paper’s own notation. The equality stated earlier in Section 4 for graph embeddings is ρ0(gcr^(t)) = ρ0(gwl_1^(t)), not vwl. This is probably a simple v/g typo, but it occurs in a formal universality consequence and should be corrected to avoid confusing vertex and graph separation notions.
